# FRAMEWORK PDF CAPTIONS — eth-drought 2020-12-07
# framework_doc: https://www.unocha.org/attachments/2f14d0f3-3153-40f9-8259-6fc93ce274d2/AA%20Framework_Ethiopia_2020_drought.pdf
# pages: 28 · model: sonnet · via: claude headless
# Auto-generated descriptions of the PDF's visuals (maps/charts/tables). PUBLIC.
------------------------------------------------------------
--- page 1 ---
Cover page: "ANTICIPATORY ACTION PLAN For Drought in Ethiopia" — living document during the 2020-2021 pilot phase, version 07 December 2020. No figure.

--- page 2 ---
Table of contents listing 7 sections: 1. Introduction, 2. Ethiopia Crisis Timeline, 3. Forecast and Triggers, 4. Pre-identified Anticipatory Actions, 5. Pre-committed Financing, 6. Execution of the Plan, 7. Evaluation and Learning; plus three Annexes (I cluster intervention sheets, II seasonal action calendars, III pre-committed funding). No figure.

--- page 3 ---
Section 1 Introduction — Drought in Ethiopia and Rationale for Anticipatory Action. Key historical events: 1984-5 drought caused >0.5 million deaths; 2002/2011 failures led to 11.3 million food insecure and 4.5 million needing food aid; 2015-16 El Niño led to >10 million people needing assistance, followed by 2017 Indian Ocean Dipole drought with 8.5 million needing relief. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 4 ---
Section 1 continued — Objectives of Anticipatory Action. Diagram: "What does traditional humanitarian response look like?" showing linear sequence Severe Crisis → Assessments → Planning & prioritising → Monitoring & Evaluation → Aid Delivery. Text describes three core AA components: (i) robust forecasting for trigger/parameter identification, (ii) pre-agreed activities, (iii) pre-committed financing. Two stated goals: (1) prevent and reduce human suffering; (2) protect hard-won development gains.

--- page 5 ---
Diagrams illustrating the AA model. (1) Collective anticipatory action sequence: Robust Forecasting & Baseline Information → Established Action Plans → Pre-scoped Financing → Anticipatory Action → Reduced Crisis. (2) Rationale icons: Reduce humanitarian impact & response cost / More dignified / Protect development gains. (3) Impact reduction curve chart: two curves — traditional humanitarian response peaks higher; anticipatory action intervenes earlier and reduces peak crisis. Core plan principles introduced: Multi-sectoral (bundled delivery of services maximises synergies).

--- page 6 ---
Core principles continued — Centrality of protection, Accountability to Affected Populations (AAP), Localization, Cash where possible, Development Co-Benefits. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 7 ---
Linkages to the Humanitarian Programme Cycle — explains how anticipatory action fits alongside HRP, ERP contingency planning, and CERF Rapid Response Window / Ethiopia Humanitarian Fund. Three ERP pathways described: (a) include actions in HRP; (b) separate costed contingency plan; (c) anticipatory action framework activated before impact materialises. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 8 ---
Section 2: Ethiopia Crisis Timeline — Understanding drought. References NOAA four drought types: meteorological, hydrological, agricultural, socioeconomic. Humanitarian impact pathway begins with water deficit → disease (cholera within 2-3 weeks, measles within 3-4 months) and school disruption, commercial water cost forcing debt. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 9 ---
Continued crisis timeline — Water deficit for agricultural production (reduced yield, reduced labour income, food price rises, malnutrition); water/fodder shortage for animal consumption (livestock body condition decline, reproduction falls, disease outbreak risk, export bans). Crop prices and labour seasonality noted. Section 3 Forecast and Triggers introduced: two-part trigger (food security + climatic). Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 10 ---
Trigger Definition — two-step mechanism:
FOOD SECURITY (IPC/Global-IPC, 3-month horizon): BOTH conditions met — (1) ≥20% of population in ≥1 ADMIN3 region projected at IPC4+; AND (2) ≥30% of ADMIN3 population in IPC3+ projected to increase by ≥5 percentage points in ADMIN1 population.
DROUGHT (ICPAC/NMME/NIME/IRI, seasonal forecasts): BOTH conditions met — (1) ≥50% probability of below-average rainfall from ≥2 seasonal rainfall forecasts; AND (2) drought named as driver of deterioration in food security report.
Mechanism specs: framework activates for single region meeting threshold but does not dictate response geography; funding not released more than once per year even if threshold crossed multiple times; mechanism evaluated as part of pilot. Data sources: FEWS NET, IPC, Global-IPC, ICPAC, NMME, NIME, IRI, FEWSNET.

--- page 11 ---
Section 4: Pre-identified Anticipatory Actions — Priority interventions. Two categories: (A) activities preventing/mitigating potential impact; (B) activities stabilising/protecting vulnerable people and containing worsening conditions. Priority actions described:
- Sustain clean water provision for animals and humans: rehabilitating water points, optimal water capture and storage, safe hygiene practices.
- Sustain food production for human consumption: distribute drought-tolerant short-season seed varieties (green gram highlighted), reduce severe crop losses. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 12 ---
Continued pre-identified actions:
- Sustain animal productivity: livestock supportive treatment campaign (reduces disease spread, boosts body condition); distribution of supplementary feeds (particularly milk yield).
- Stabilise purchasing power: unconditional cash transfers to vulnerable households to meet immediate needs and invest in productive activities.
- Rapidly respond to communicable disease: stand up/re-activate Rapid Response Teams (RRTs); causal link between drought and acute watery diarrhoea, measles mortality.
- Keep children in school: cash for school fees/priority purchases; water supplies in schools reduce dropout (especially adolescent girls; protection co-benefit).
- Improve nutrition practices: MIYCF promotion to protect nutritional status of children <5. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 13 ---
Continued pre-identified actions:
- Support community-based conflict mitigation: drought intensifies land/water disputes; capacitate traditional arbitration mechanisms; address displacement of excluded communities.
- Distribute essential dignity items to women and girls: continuous access to hygiene/protection goods without resorting to negative coping.
- Mainstream protection including child protection and GBV prevention: awareness training for AA implementers; rapid risk assessments; referral updates. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 14 ---
Summary table of interventions and key information — clusters (Agriculture, WASH, Nutrition, Education, Health, Protection) with columns: Anticipatory Actions, Lead time, Cost Estimate. Table uses colour coding for cost ranges (green=lower, yellow=medium, red=higher). Lead times range from ~1 month (cash, NFI distribution) to 2-6 months (water scheme rehabilitation). Key point: cost estimates and lead times set for pre-deployment planning across six sectors.

--- page 15 ---
Seasonal calendars of anticipatory actions and ensuring readiness for timely implementation. Text describes readiness considerations for clusters: how priority needs will be met, whether activities can be activated (have not missed the window), who the partners are, what operational cross-cutting issues exist. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 16 ---
Section 6: Execution of the Plan — Activation steps:
Step 1 Trigger validation: 1-2 days; RC/HC decides; OCHA coordinates, Government and CLA consulted; key questions include country-level data disagreeing with projections and whether pre-agreed activities are still appropriate.
Step 2 Rapid analysis of projected impact: 3 days; RC/HC with support of OCHA; key questions include scope/scale of people affected, which pre-agreed activities can be activated and locations, how much pre-committed plan will contribute.
Step 3 Re-calibration of Anticipatory Action Plan: 3 days; RC/HC with OCHA; meetings include ICCG, DRMTWG, EHCT. Key questions on adjustments to pre-agreed plan. No figure.

--- page 17 ---
Continued execution — Implementation. Anticipatory action plan implemented by Humanitarian Country Team in collaboration with Disaster Risk Management Technical Working Group (NDRMTWG) and development partners. Intersectoral coordination by OCHA; national/regional government coordination critical. Bullet points on key re-calibration questions (adjustments to pre-agreed plan, minimising impact, which organisations best placed). Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 18 ---
Section 7: Evaluation and Learning — two components:
Component 1 (Action Learning Reviews): structured according to three hypotheses co-developed by CDP and OCHA; 8-10 stakeholder interviews at HQ and country level (50/50 representation).
Component 2 (Qualitative data collection): independent evaluator gathers data on how to do AA in 8-10 interviews.
Final Lessons Learned Report (PPT): cumulative across action learning reviews plus key informant interviews and document review.
Agency-specific monitoring: minimum indicators agreed upon by partners, shared with OCHA.
Independent evaluation: Theory of Change-based; ad-hoc pilot learning; monitoring/evaluation committee chaired by OCHA. No figure.

--- page 19 ---
Annexes introduction — Annex I: Cluster intervention sheets (available upon request); Annex II: Anticipatory Actions seasonal calendars (pages 20-26), covering four geographical areas and rainy seasons: Kiremt, Belg, Gu, Karan, Deyr; Annex III: Pre-committed funding overview and donor release processes. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 20 ---
Seasonal calendar — Western agriculture areas, Kiremt rains. Months Jan–Dec. Legend: $$ = Disbursement of CERF funding; dark green = Lead time; medium green = Provision of assistance/core implementation; light green = Post Distribution Monitoring.
Forecast publication markers: ICPAC (Feb, May, Aug, Nov); IPC (Feb, May); FEWSNET (Feb, Apr–May, Aug, Sep–Oct, Dec).
Seasons: Kiremt rains Jul–Sep (blue); Meher planting & harvest Apr–Nov (orange/yellow); Lean season Jun–Jul (red).
Cluster disbursement triggers ($$ = CERF release month):
- Agriculture: Farming inputs & Cash $$ Feb; Animal health treatment $$ Feb; Livestock feed & Cash $$ Mar.
- Nutrition: MIYCN $$ May; MUAC screening $$ May.
- WASH: Rehabilitation of non-functioning water schemes $$ Jan; Distribution of NFIs $$ Jan; Hygiene promotion $$ Jan.
- Health: Strengthening RRTs $$ Aug.
- Protection: Traditional arbitration mechanisms $$ Feb; Child protection awareness $$ Aug; Mainstreaming GBV & child protection $$ Apr; Dignity kits $$ May.
- Education: Water provision in schools $$ Aug; Cash for education $$ Aug.

Seasons: Belg rains Feb–May (blue); Belg planting & harvest Mar–Jun (orange/yellow); Lean season Nov–Dec (red).

Cluster disbursement triggers:
- Agriculture: Farming inputs & Cash $$ Oct; Animal health treatment $$ Oct; Livestock feed & cash $$ Nov.
- Nutrition: MIYCN $$ Nov; MUAC screening $$ Nov.
- WASH: Rehabilitation $$ Sep; Distribution of NFIs $$ Aug; Hygiene promotion $.
- Education: Water provision in schools $$ Nov; Cash for education $$ Nov.
- Health: RRTs $$ May.
- Protection: Traditional arbitration $$ Oct; Child protection awareness $$ Apr; Mainstreaming GBV $$ Nov; Dignity kits $$ Jan.

--- page 22 ---

--- page 23 ---
Seasonal calendar — Northern pastoral areas, Gu & Diraac/Sugum rains. Months Jan–Dec.
Seasons: Gu & Diraac/Sugum rains Feb–May (blue); Lean season Feb–Mar (red).
Cluster disbursement triggers (single $ denomination, smaller budget):
- Agriculture: Farming inputs & cash (Gu/Diraac) $ Nov; Animal health $ Nov; Livestock feed $ Dec.
- Nutrition: MIYCN $ Oct; MUAC (Gu/Diraac) $ Nov.
- WASH: Rehabilitation $ Sep; Distribution NFIs $ Oct; Hygiene $ Oct.
- Education: Water provision $ Nov; Cash $ Nov.
- Health: RRTs $ Apr.
- Protection: Traditional arbitration $ Nov; Child protection $ Apr; Mainstreaming GBV/CP $ Nov; Dignity kits $ Jan.

--- page 24 ---

Seasons: Karan/Karma rains Aug–Oct (blue); Lean season Feb–Mar (red).

- Agriculture: Farming inputs & cash (Karan/Karma) $ Mar; Animal health $ Mar; Livestock feed $ Mar.
- Nutrition: MIYCN $ Oct; MUAC (Karan/Karma) $ May.
- WASH: Rehabilitation $ Jan; Distribution NFIs $ Jan; Hygiene $ Feb.
- Education: Water in schools $ Aug; Cash for education $ Aug.
- Health: RRTs $ Nov.
- Protection: Traditional arbitration $ Mar; Child protection awareness $ Aug; Mainstreaming GBV/CP $ May; Dignity kits $ Jun.

--- page 25 ---

Seasons: Gu/Genna rains Mar–May (blue); Lean season Jan–Feb (red).

- WASH: Rehabilitation $$ Aug; Distribution NFIs $$ Oct; Hygiene $$ Oct.

--- page 26 ---
Seasonal calendar — Southern pastoral areas, Deyr rains. Months Jan–Dec.
Seasons: Deyr/Hageya rains Oct–Dec (blue); Lean season Feb–Mar (red).
Cluster disbursement triggers (single $ denomination):
- Agriculture: Farming inputs & cash (Deyr) $ Jun; Animal health $ Jun; Livestock feed $ Jun.
- Nutrition: MIYCN $ Nov; MUAC $ Dec.
- WASH: Rehabilitation $ May; Distribution NFIs $ May; Hygiene $ May.
- Education: Water in schools $ Jul; Cash for education $ Jul.
- Health: RRTs $ Nov.
- Protection: Traditional arbitration $ Jun; Child protection $ Oct; Mainstreaming GBV/CP $ Aug; Dignity kits $ Sep.

--- page 27 ---
Annex III: Pre-committed funding and its release processes. For the initial pilot phase, pre-committed donor is the Central Emergency Relief Fund (CERF), established by UN General Assembly in 2005. CERF principles: simple, quick, predictable; builds on existing structures; increases responsibility and accountability. Anticipatory action should reinforce existing functioning structures, not build new ones. Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 28 ---
CERF funding release process — two steps:
(a) Preparation and submission of CERF application: 5-6 days; OCHA office compiles information from anticipatory action plan and rapid impact analysis; cluster coordinators and HC involved; HC submits to CERF secretariat (anticipatory action plan annexed to application).
(b) Due diligence and disbursement approval: 10 days; CERF secretariat reviews; OCHA Ethiopia and agencies ensure proposals adhere to CERF regulations; funding disbursed following approval by Emergency Relief Coordinator. Pre-condition: trigger thresholds reached and validated; rapid analysis of projected impact completed; action plan re-calibrated. Narrative text, no figure.
